gbtk
  • gbtk package
  • View page source

gbtk package

Submodules

gbtk.crystaltools module

Module containing a number of helper functions for handling and visualising crystal structures

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
gbtk.crystaltools.calculate_corners_ppp(cellvectors, repeats)

Calculate the coordinates of the corners of a parallelipiped defined by three vectors.

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors defining edges of parallelipiped

  • repeats (ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)) – min and max of repeats in three basis vector directions (see bounding box functions)

Returns:

cartesian coordinates of corners

Return type:

ndarray((8,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.calculate_edges_ppp()

Define the edges of a parallelipiped consistent with the ordering in calculate_corners_ppp function.

Returns:

edges of parallelipiped specified as pairs of integers indexing corners at either end of edge

Return type:

ndarray((8,3),dtype=int)

gbtk.crystaltools.check_common_factors(indices, limit=100)

Check the list of integers for a common factor up to the value of limit.

Parameters:

  • indices (list of integers) – list of integers to check for common factors

  • limit (int, optional) – maximum value of common factor to try, defaults to 100

Returns:

True if a common factor <=limit exists

Return type:

boolean

gbtk.crystaltools.components_in_basis(vector, basis)

Calculate the components of the N-dimensional vector in the given basis.

Parameters:

  • vector (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – cartesian vector to express

  • basis (ndarray((N,N),dtype=float)) – basis vectors in cartesian space

Returns:

components of vector in basis

Return type:

ndarray(N,dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.fill_box(supercellvectors, cellvectors, basis, repeats, basis_types=None, overfill=False)

Return a list of atom coordinates filling a box defined by supercellvectors, using a set of lattice vectors given by cellvectors and a basis. Repeats contains the search bounds required to fill the box.

Parameters:

  • supercellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors describing supercell to fill

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors describing cell to be used to fill supercell

  • basis (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – positions of atoms within the unit cell

  • repeats (ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)) – min and max values in direction of each cell vector in order to tile supercell

  • basis_types (ndarray(M,dtype=int), optional) – array of integers specifiying type of atoms within unit cell, defaults to None

  • overfill (bool, optional) – set to True to overfill the box. WARNING! useful for visualisations, but will probably break periodicity with periodic boundaries! Defaults to False

Raises:

RuntimeError – _description_

Returns:

(number of atoms, coordinates, types) or (number of atoms, coordinates)

Return type:

(int, ndarray((N,3),dtype=float), ndarray(N,dtype=float)) or (int, ndarray((N,3),dtype=float))

gbtk.crystaltools.find_bonds(cell, r, rnn, wrap=False)

Return an array of atom pairs that are closer together than a specified separation. Only considers each pair once.

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – array describing cell (required in order to account for periodic boundaries if wrap=True)

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – atom coordinates

  • rnn (float) – cut-off distance for determining neighbours

  • wrap (bool, optional) – flag to select if periodic boundaries applied, defaults to False

Returns:

array of indices of atom pairs

Return type:

ndarray((M,2),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.find_neighbours(cell, r, r_nn, wrap=True, max_neigh=16)

Find a list of neighbours and their relative positions for all the atoms in an array of position coordinates. Use find_neighbours_single() to get this information for a single atom only.

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description. Required in order to treat wrapping at boundaries

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates to examine

  • r_nn (float) – Cut-off distance within which to treat atoms as neighbours

  • wrap (bool, optional) – Flag to indicate if boundaries are periodic, defaults to True

  • max_neigh (int, optional) – Maximum nunber of neighbours to record, defaults to 16. Ensure this is large enough to avoid missing neighbours within r_nn

Returns:

Number of neighbours of each atom, indices of these neighbouring atoms in input array, distance to neighbours, displacement vectors of neighbours

Return type:

ndarray((N),dtype=int), ndarray((N,max_neigh),dtype=int), ndarray((N,max_neigh),dtype=float), ndarray((N,max_neigh,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.find_neighbours_single(atom, cell, r, r_nn, wrap=True, max_neigh=16)

Find a list of neighbours and their relative positions for a specified atom in an array of position coordinates. Use find_neighbours() to get this information for all atoms in the array.

Parameters:

  • atom (int) – Index of atom of interest

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description. Required in order to treat wrapping at boundaries

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates to examine

  • r_nn (float) – Cut-off distance within which to treat atoms as neighbours

  • wrap (bool, optional) – Flag to indicate if boundaries are periodic, defaults to True

  • max_neigh (int, optional) – Maximum nunber of neighbours to record, defaults to 16. Ensure this is large enough to avoid missing neighbours within r_nn

Returns:

Number of neighbours of atom, indices of these neighbouring atoms in input array, distance to neighbours, displacement vectors of neighbours

Return type:

int, ndarray((max_neigh),dtype=int), ndarray((max_neigh),dtype=float), ndarray((max_neigh,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.get_bounding_box(boxvectors, cellvectors)

Calculate the smallest bounding box in the specified basis that circumscribes a parallelipiped.

Parameters:

  • boxvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors (in rows of an array) defining a parallelipiped

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – basis in which bounding box is to be specified

Returns:

max and min of bounding box in three basis vector directions

Return type:

ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.get_interstices(cell, r, pad, r_cluster)

Take a cell specification and list of atom positions and find the interstices via a voronoi tesselation. The atom array is augmented by an amount specified via <pad> on all faces and edges to mimic periodic boundaries in a way that Voronoi can handle. The detected vertices of the voronoi mesh are then pruned if they are closer than <r_cluster>.

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description. Required in order to treat wrapping at boundaries

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates to examine

  • pad (float) – Width of padding region to apply at all surfaces of supercell

  • r_cluster (float) – Distance below which two voronoi points are considered to coincide and are pruned

Returns:

Array of coordinates of interstices

Return type:

ndarray((M,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.get_ortho_bounding_box(boxvectors)

Calculate the smallest orthorhombic bounding box (aligned with the cartesian axes) that circumscribes a parallelipiped.

Parameters:

boxvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors (in rows of an array) defining a parallelipiped

Returns:

max and min of bounding box in three cartesian directions

Return type:

ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.get_repeats(supercellvectors, cellvectors, axis=None, angle=None)

Return the number of repeats of a cell along each lattice vector required to fill a supercell box with atoms. Optionally the cell can be rotated (as specified by an axis-angle combination) prior to filling the supercell.

Parameters:

  • supercellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors describing supercell to fill

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors describing cell to be used to fill supercell

  • axis (ndarray(3,dtype=float), optional) – axis about which to rotate the cell, defaults to None

  • angle (float, optional) – angle by which to rotate the cell, defaults to None

Returns:

max and min of bounding box in three basis vector directions

Return type:

ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.get_voronoi_volumes(cell, r, pad)

Take a cell specification and list of atom positions and find the volumes associated with each atom via a voronoi tesselation. The atom array is augmented by an amount specified via <pad> on all faces and edges to mimic periodic boundaries in a way that Voronoi can handle.

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description. Required in order to treat wrapping at boundaries

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates to examine

  • pad (float) – Width of padding region to apply at all surfaces of supercell

Returns:

Array of atom (voronoi cell) volumes

Return type:

ndarray((N),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.indices_in_basis(vector, basis)

Express the direction of the given N-dimensional vector in the given basis such that the components are integer.

Parameters:

  • vector (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – cartesian vector to express

  • basis (ndarray((N,N),dtype=float)) – basis vectors in cartesian space

Returns:

components of direction of vector in basis

Return type:

ndarray(N,dtype=int)

gbtk.crystaltools.integer_indices(vector, limit=10000, tol=0.001)

Return a multiple of the given N-dimensional vector such that the components are integer.

Parameters:

  • vector (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – cartesian vector to express

  • limit (int, optional) – maximum multiple to consider, defaults to 10000

  • tol (float, optional) – Tolerance used to determine when a component is integer (max rounding error), defaults to 1e-3

Returns:

multiple of input vestor such that components are integer

Return type:

ndarray(N,dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.is_in_cell(cellvectors, pos, tol=1e-06, overfill=False)

Test whether a poition vector lies within a cell (specified via edges of a parallelipiped).

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – parallelipiped to test

  • pos (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – position to test

  • tol (float, optional) – tolerance for testing, defaults to 1e-6

  • overfill (bool, optional) – set to True to overfill the box. WARNING! useful for visualisations, but will probably break periodicity with periodic boundaries! Defaults to False

Returns:

is the position within the cell?

Return type:

boolean

gbtk.crystaltools.lammps_box_to_ppd(box_cell)

Take a supercell as specified in a lammps input file and convert to three edge vectors of a parallelipiped.

Parameters:

box_cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – array describing cell in lammps input format

Returns:

cell described as array of edge vectors of a parallelipiped

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.lammps_dump_to_ppd(dump_cell)

Take a supercell as specified in a lammps dump file and convert to three edge vectors of a parallelipiped.

Parameters:

dump_cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – array describing cell in lammps dump format

Returns:

cell described as array of edge vectors of a parallelipiped

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.locate_nearest_atom(r0, r)

return the index and position of the atom within an array of atom coordinates nearest to a test point.

Parameters:

  • r0 (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – coordinate of test point

  • r (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – coordinates of atoms

Returns:

index of closest atom, coordinates of closest atom

Return type:

int, ndarray((3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.mirror_boundary_atoms(cell, atom_data, tol=0.02)

Augment atom list so that atoms on boundaries and corners are reproduced on opposite side (a cosmetic adjustment).

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – cell

  • atom_data (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – array of coordinates of atoms

  • tol (float, optional) – tolerance to determine if an atom is on the boundary surface of the cell, defaults to 0.02

Returns:

array of coordinates of atoms augmented with copies of atoms on the cell surface

Return type:

ndarray((>=N,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.multiply_cell(cell, r, mults)

Take a cell specification and list of atom positions and return a larger cell with integer numbers of copies of the original positions. If other attributes are included in the array r, these will also be replicated.

Parameters:

  • cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description. Required in order to treat wrapping at boundaries

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates to examine

  • mults – Number of multiples of cell to return in each of the three cell directions

Returns:

Enlarged supercell cell description, Augmented array of atom coordinates

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float), ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.read_lammps_dump(file)

Read a lammps dump file and return the number of atoms, the supercell specification and a subset of atom data. (Warning! This is not a well written or general purpose parser for Lammps dump files. It will work if the format matches that expected, but may well fall over if not!)

Parameters:

file (str) – Name of file to read

Returns:

Number of atoms, supercell cell description, Array of atom coordinates (plus Type, ID, PE if in file)

Return type:

int, ndarray((3,3),dtype=float), ndarray((N,M),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.read_lammps_input(file)

Read structure and coordinates from a Lammps input fiule and return the number of atoms, the supercell specification and a subset of atom data. (Warning! This is not a well written or general purpose parser for Lammps input files. It will work if the format matches that expected, but may well fall over if not!)

Parameters:

file (str) – Name of file to read

Returns:

Number of atoms, supercell cell description, Array of atom coordinates, ID and Type

Return type:

int, ndarray((3,3),dtype=float), ndarray((N,5),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.rotate_cell(cellvectors, axis, angle)

Rotate a set of cell vectors according to a psecified axis and angle.

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – description of cell in terms of basis vectors

  • axis (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – axis

  • angle (float) – angle, in radians

Returns:

rotated cell in terms of basis vectors

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.rotation_matrix(axis, theta)

Calculate and return a rotation matrix from an axis-angle combination.

Parameters:

  • axis (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – axis

  • theta (float) – angle, in radians

Returns:

rotation matrix in cartesian space

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.rotation_matrix_into_direction(vector, direction)

Rotation matrix required to rotate a given vector into a given direction.

Parameters:

  • vector (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – vector in cartesian space, vector to rotate

  • direction (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – vector in cartesian space, target direction to rotate input vector into

Returns:

rotation matrix in cartesian space

Return type:

ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.translate_coordinates(r, dr, cellvectors)

Translate all the coordinates in the array r by the vector dr, then rewrap to the box specified by cellvectors.

Parameters:

  • r (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – original positions

  • dr (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – translation vector

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – parallelipiped to test

Returns:

translated positions

Return type:

ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.vector_in_basis(components, basis_vectors)

Return an N-dimensional vector specified by N components as multiples of the N basis vectors.

Parameters:

  • components (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – multiples of the basis vectors

  • basis (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – basis vectors in cartesian space

Returns:

cartesian vector

Return type:

ndarray(N,dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.vis_data_box(corners, edges)

Return data for visualising a box in a plotly scatter plot.

Parameters:

  • corners (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – array of coordinates of corners

  • edges (ndarray((N,2),dtype=int)) – array holding starting and ending corner indices for edges

Returns:

lists of coordinates of points spearated by cartesian direction

Return type:

(list[N,2], list[N,2], list[N,2])

gbtk.crystaltools.vis_data_box_ppp(cellvectors, repeats)

Return a list of points for visualising a parallelipiped in a plotly scatter plot.

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – vectors defining edges of cell

  • repeats (ndarray((3,2),dtype=float)) – number of repeats of cell

Returns:

lists of coordinates of points spearated by cartesian direction

Return type:

(list[N,2], list[N,2], list[N,2])

gbtk.crystaltools.vis_data_lines(points)

Return data for visualising a series of line segments in a plotly scatter plot segments run from points[i,0,:] to points[i,1,:].

Parameters:

points (ndarray((N,2,3),dtype=float)) – array of starting and ending points in pairs defining line segments

Returns:

lists of coordinates of points spearated by cartesian direction

Return type:

(list[N,2], list[N,2], list[N,2])

gbtk.crystaltools.vis_data_vectors(points, vectors)

Return data for visualising a set of vectors in a plotly scatter plot vectors run from points[i,:] to points[i,:]+vectors[i,:].

Parameters:

  • points (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – array of starting points for vectors

  • vectors (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – array of vectors

Returns:

lists of coordinates of points spearated by cartesian direction

Return type:

(list[N,2], list[N,2], list[N,2])

gbtk.crystaltools.wrap_to_cell(cellvectors, pos, tol=1e-06, overfill=False)

Wrap a coordinate into a cell assuming periodic boundary conditions.

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – parallelipiped to test

  • pos (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – position to test

  • tol (float, optional) – tolerance for testing, defaults to 1e-6

  • overfill (bool, optional) – set to True to overfill the box. WARNING! useful for visualisations, but will probably break periodicity with periodic boundaries! Defaults to False

Returns:

wrapped position

Return type:

ndarray(3,dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.wrap_vector_to_cell(cellvectors, vec)

Wrap a vector into a cell assuming periodic boundary conditions.

Parameters:

  • cellvectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – cell to wrap to

  • vec (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – original vector

Returns:

wrapped vector

Return type:

ndarray(3,dtype=float)

gbtk.crystaltools.write_lammps(supercell, atom_pos, atom_type=None, filename='lammps.txt', num_types=1)

Write out a supercell in Lammps format

Parameters:

  • supercell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description

  • atom_pos (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates

  • atom_type (ndarray((N),dtype=int), optional, all atom types set to 1 if not supplied) – Array of integers indicating atom type, defaults to None

  • filename (str, optional) – name of file to write, defaults to ‘lammps.txt’

  • num_types (int, optional) – number of atom types in file, defaults to 1

gbtk.crystaltools.write_poscar(supercell, atom_pos, filename='POSCAR', type_string=None, constraints=None)

Write out a supercell in Vasp POSCAR format

Parameters:

  • supercell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell cell description

  • atom_pos (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Array of atom coordinates

  • filename (str, optional) – Name of file to write, defaults to ‘lammps.txt’

  • type_string (str, optional) – String to indicate numbers of atoms in Vasp format, defaults to None, resulting in all atoms of single species

  • constraints (N*3 multi-dimensional list of strings, optional) – Constraints to apply to individual atoms, defaults to None, resulting in T T T for all atoms

gbtk.csl module

Module to handle definitions of coincidence site lattices

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.csl.CSL(latticetype, lengths=None, angles=None)

Bases: object

A csl holds details of the misorientation axis-angle combination for a grain boundary. It also contains the csl basis vectors.

Variables:

  • lattice (object) – Lattice object containg description of underlying lattice

  • axis (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Misorientation axis in multiples of lattice cell vectors

  • axis_cartesian (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Misorientation axis in cartesian space

  • angle (float) – misorientation angle in radians

  • angle_indices (ndarray(2,dtype=integer)) – Values of m,n specifying misorentation angle

  • misorientation_rotation (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Rotation matrix of 0.5*angle about misorientation axis

  • search (object) – spatialsearch object to faciliate search for CSL basis vectors

  • csl_vectors_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Vectors of CSL basis (in rows) in black half of bicrystal in multiples of lattice vectors

  • csl_vectors_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Vectors of CSL basis (in rows) in white half of bicrystal in multiples of lattice vectors

  • csl_vectors_cartesian_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Vectors of CSL basis (in rows) in black half of bicrystal in cartesian space

  • csl_vectors_cartesian_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Vectors of CSL basis (in rows) in black half of bicrystal in cartesian space

  • dsc_vectors_cartesian_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float))

  • dsc_vectors_cartesian_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float))

  • dsc_vector_fractions (ndarray(3,dtype=float))

  • cell_volume (float) – Volume of CSL cell in cartesian space units

  • cell_volume_lattice (float) – Volume of CSL cell in units of lattice unit cells

  • cell_volume_atoms (integer) – Number of atoms in CSL cell

  • debug (boolean) – Flag set to True if debug information requested

  • search_enabled (boolean) – Flag to indicate if spatialsearch object is initialised

  • angle_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if misorientation angle is set

  • axis_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if misorientation axis is set

  • csl_basis_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if csl_basis has been calculated (and csl_vectors_black, etc. populated)

  • misorientation_rotation_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if misorientation_rotation matrix is populated

__init__(latticetype, lengths=None, angles=None)

Initialise an empty csl with the specified lattice type.

Parameters:

  • latticetype (string) – Lattice type, passed to Lattice module

  • lengths (list of floats, optional) – Lengths of unit cell vectors, passed to Lattice module, defaults to None

  • angles (list of floats, optional) – Angles between unit cell vectors, passed to Lattice module, defaults to None

enable_search(maxradius=5)

Populate an array of search vectors using spatialsearch module.

Parameters:

maxradius (int, optional) – Maximum searhc radius, defaults to 5

find_csl_basis(tol=0.001)

Find a set of basis vectors suitable for defining the csl by finding 3 non-colinear points in CSL lattice i.e. lattice points commont to black and white lattices. These vectors are recorded both in the original crystal lattice and in the cartesian basis in the original orientation.

Parameters:

tol (float, optional) – Tolerance used to define when two vectors are equivalent (max deviation of normalised dot product from unity), defaults to CSL_TOL

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Misorientation axis and angle must be set before finding csl basis” if either misorientation axis or angle not yet set

  • RuntimeError – “Misorientation rotation matrix must be calculated before finding csl basis - callfind_misorientation_rotation_matrix()” if rotation matrix for misorientation not yet calculated

  • RuntimeError – “Search not enabled, please call enable_search() to find csl basis” is a spatialsearch object has not been instantiated

Returns:

Flag for success in finding basis

Return type:

boolean

find_dsc_lattice(tol=0.001)

Finds the DSC (displacement shift complete) lattice vectors for the CSL and populates instance variables to store the details.

Parameters:

tol (float, optional) – Tolerance used to define when two vectors are equivalent (max deviation of normalised dot product from unity), defaults to CSL_TOL

Raises:

RuntimeError – “CSL basis must be found before calculating DSC lattice” if attemtping to find DSC lattice before CSL basis calculated

find_misorientation_rotation_matrix()

Find rotation matrix to rotate each crystal about misorientation axis (by half misorientation angle).

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Misorientation axis must be set before calculating misorientation rotation” if misorientation axis not already set

  • RuntimeError – “Misorientation angle must be set before calculating misorientation rotation” if misorientation angle not already set

set_angle(angle)

Set the misorientation angle directly with a value in radians.

Parameters:

angle (float) – Angle specified in radians

set_angle_mn(m, n)

Set the misorientation angle via a pair of integers. Only works for cubic lattice types.

Parameters:

  • m (integer) – first parameter

  • n (integer) – second parameter

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Axis must be specified before setting angle using this method” if axis not already specified

  • RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + self.lattice.lattice_type + “ not supported.” if an unsupported lattice type is specified

set_axis(axis)

Define misorientation axis for CSL.

Parameters:

axis ([int,int,int]) – Components of axis specified in multiples of the lattice cell vectors

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Misorientation axis vector must have 3 components” if wrong number of components specified

set_debug()

Turn on debug info.

unset_debug()

Turn off debug info

visualise_2d_rotated(axis=-1)

Generate a plot showing a 2D projection of the CSL.

Parameters:

axis (int, optional) – Determines the vector down which the structure is projected (viewed). -1 selects misoreientation axis, [0,1,2] select one of the CSL vectors, defaults to -1

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Invalid choice for projection axis”, if an invalid value for the axis is specified

visualise_3d()

Generate a plot showing the CSL.

visualise_3d_rotated()

Generate a plot showing the CSL in 3D.

gbtk.csl.calculate_cosine(h, k, l, m, n, lattice_type='fcc')

Calculate the cosine of misorientation angle, expressed as the components of a fraction in a tuple for the grain boundary from the axis [h,k,l] and the integers m,n in a specified lattice type. Currently only implemented for cubic lattice types.

Parameters:

  • h (integer) – First component of misorientation axis

  • k (integer) – Second component of misorientation axis

  • l (integer) – Third component of misorientation axis

  • m (integer) – First parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • n (integer) – Second parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • lattice_type (str, optional) – _description_, defaults to ‘fcc’

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + self.lattice.lattice_type + “ not supported.” if an unsupported lattice type is specified

Returns:

Cosine of the misorientation angle expressed as the components of a fraction in a tuple (numerator,denominator)

Return type:

(integer,integer)

gbtk.csl.calculate_sigma(h, k, l, m, n, lattice_type='fcc')

Calculate the value of sigma for the grain boundary from the axis [h,k,l] and the integers m,n in a specified lattice type. Currently only implemented for cubic lattice types.

Parameters:

  • h (integer) – First component of misorientation axis

  • k (integer) – Second component of misorientation axis

  • l (integer) – Third component of misorientation axis

  • m (integer) – First parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • n (integer) – Second parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • lattice_type (str, optional) – lattice type, defaults to ‘fcc’

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + self.lattice.lattice_type + “ not supported.” if an unsupported lattice type is specified

Returns:

Sigma value

Return type:

integer

gbtk.csl.calculate_theta(h, k, l, m, n, lattice_type='fcc')

Calculate the misorientation angle for the grain boundary from the axis [h,k,l] and the integers m,n in a specified lattice type. Currently only implemented for cubic lattice types.

Parameters:

  • h (integer) – First component of misorientation axis

  • k (integer) – Second component of misorientation axis

  • l (integer) – Third component of misorientation axis

  • m (integer) – First parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • n (integer) – Second parameter specifying misorientation angle

  • lattice_type (str, optional) – _description_, defaults to ‘fcc’

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + self.lattice.lattice_type + “ not supported.” if an unsupported lattice type is specified

Returns:

Misorientation angle in radians

Return type:

float

gbtk.csl.write_csl_catalogue(folder, lattice_type, axis, limit, ext='df', include_basis=False, atom_limit=None)

Write out a catalogue of possible angles and sigma values for a given grain boundary axis. Currently only works for cubic lattices.

Parameters:

  • folder (string) – Location of folder to write catalogue to

  • lattice_type (string) – Lattice type

  • axis ([int,int,int] or ndarray(3, dtype=int)) – Misorientation axis to consider

  • limit (int) – Limit to use foir search in the form of maximum valed for m and n

  • ext (str, optional) – File extension, defaults to ‘df’

  • include_basis (bool, optional) – Flag to control whether CSL basis included in output (significantly increases runtime), defaults to False

  • atom_limit (int, optional) – Write out only those CSLs with fewer atoms than this, defaults to None

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Cataloguing functionality not available for “ + lattice_type + “ lattice type”, if cataloguing for an unsupported lattice is requested

  • RuntimeError – “Unsupported file type: “ + ext + “. Extention must be one of: df, csv, xlsx”, if an unsupported file type is specified

  • RuntimeError – “Atom limit can only be applied if include_basis=true”, if atom_limit is set, but inclide_basis is not set to True

Returns:

A catalogue of the possible CSL grain boundaries for the given axis within the limits set

Return type:

Pandas dataframe

gbtk.gbcalculation module

Module to create files for calculations of grain boundary properties and aggregate the results. Currently only contains functionality for grain boundary gamma surfaces

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
gbtk.gbcalculation.gamma_surface_analyse(shiftrange, shiftres, expansionrange, expansionres, path, energycol=4, final=True, tol=0.001)

Reads the results of the calculations run in a folder structure generated by gamma_surface_build(). The set-up must mathc that used to build the folder structure.

Important! Whilst the build function will generate the input files (POSCARS) for vasp calulations, this function assumes that the results are in lammps dump files. It further assumes that the dump files one per step and are named dump.* where the asterisk is the step number. In particualr, it uses the file named dump.0

To interpolate the optimal configuration there is first a quadratic interpolation at each shift combination (each point in the gamma surface grid) to build a surface optimised for expansion at the boundary. Then this surface is quadratically interpolated in the two shift directions to find the overall optimimum. Where an insufficient range of expansion is probed and so no minimum exists in the dataset, this is flagged in the ‘completion’ column of the gamma surface files.

A number of files are written, as follows:

gamma_spec.txt: specification of the gamma surface (min, max and resolution for the shifts and expansion) and the supercell vectors and numbers of atoms.
gamma_results.txt: master file of results - unrelaxed and relaxed energies for all combinations of shifts and expansion.
gamma_unrelaxed.txt: unrelaxed gamma surface - unrelaxed energies for each combination of shifts at zero expansion (i.e. “as constructed”).
gamma_optimised.txt: optimised gamma surface - expansions and relaxed energies at each shift combination interpolated to the optimal expansion. Header contains details of the overall best shift and optimal energy, again interpolated.
gamma_best.txt:: best gamma surface - expansions and relaxed energies based on the best case (lowest relaxed energy) amongst those actually explicitly simulated, i.e. without interpolation. Header contains details of the best case.
Parameters:

  • shiftrange (ndarray((2,2),dtype=float)) – Range of values of in-plane shift to apply as min and max for each direction, one row per direction

  • shiftres (ndarray(2,dtype=int)) – Number of parts to divide the shift range into in each dimension

  • expansionrange (ndarray(2,dtype=float)) – Min and max expansion to apply at boundary

  • expansionres (ndarray(1,dtype=int)) – Number of parts to divide the expansion range into in each dimension

  • path (string) – Folder containing job directories

  • energycol (int, optional) – specifies which column in the dump files contains the total energy variable, defaults to 4

  • final (bool, optional) – Flag controls whether result of last minimisation is recorded as the relaxed energy, otherwise result of first minimisation is used. Defaults to True

  • tol (float, optional) – Not currently used, defaults to GBCALCULATION_TOL

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Shift range must have 4 components, shape (2,2)”

  • RuntimeError – “Shift resolution must have 2 components”

  • RuntimeError – “Expansion range must have 2 components”

  • RuntimeError – “Expansion resolution must have 1 component”

gbtk.gbcalculation.gamma_surface_build(supercell, shiftrange, shiftres, expansionrange, expansionres, path, filestocopy, tol=0.001, fileformat='lammps')

Generate files and folder structure for a gamma surface calculation and write a file list that can be used by a job script to execute the jobs. The necessary files common to each calculation should be present in the current folder and passed as a list via the parameter filestocopy. For a lammps job this will be a lammps job script (specifying a minimisation process) and any required empircal potential files. For a vasp job, this will be the INCAR, POTCAR, KPOINTS files.

Parameters:

  • supercell (object) – gbsupercell object defining the grain boundary supercell forming the base for the gamma surface calculatio

  • shiftrange (ndarray((2,2),dtype=float)) – Range of values of in-plane shift to apply as min and max for each direction, one row per direction

  • shiftres (ndarray(2,dtype=int)) – Number of parts to divide the shift range into in each dimension

  • expansionrange (ndarray(2,dtype=float)) – Min and max expansion to apply at boundary

  • expansionres (ndarray(1,dtype=int)) – Number of parts to divide the expansion range into in each dimension

  • path (string) – Folder in which to generate job directories

  • filestocopy (list of strings) – List of files to copy into each job directory. These files must be in the current directory and cannot include paths

  • tol (float, optional) – Not currently used, defaults to GBCALCULATION_TOL

  • fileformat (str, optional) – Type of input file to write, defaults to ‘lammps’. Values of ‘lammps’ or ‘vasp’ currently supported.

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Shift range must have 4 components, shape (2,2)”

  • RuntimeError – “Shift resolution must have 2 components”

  • RuntimeError – “Expansion range must have 2 components”

  • RuntimeError – “Expansion resolution must have 1 component”

  • RuntimeError – “Unrecognised output file format” if fileformat not supported. Values of ‘lammps’ or ‘vasp’ currently supported.

gbtk.gbsupercell module

Module to handle definitions of supercells including generating atom coordinates and supercell specifications and writing out files in various formats

Author: Chris Race
Date: 11th January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.gbsupercell.Supercell(grainboundary)

Bases: object

A Supercell holds details of the simulation cell. Details will depend on the type of supercell to be built.

Variables:

  • grainboundary (object) – Grain boundary object

  • lattice_basis_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Array holding lattice basis vectors for black cell in supercell orientation

  • lattice_basis_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Array holding lattice basis vectors for white cell in supercell orientation

  • supercell_rotation (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Rotation matrix into supercell orientation such that grain boiundary normal lies along z in cartesian space

  • supercell_unit_cell (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Supercell unit cell vectors (cartesian basis)

  • lattice_basis_black – Lattice basis vectors in supercell orientation (cartesian basis)

  • lattice_basis_white – Lattice basis vectors in supercell orientation (cartesian basis)

  • shift (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Relative shift of cells at boundary

  • boundary_plane_shift (float) – Out of plane shift of each boundary

  • expansion (float) – Amount to expand cell by at each boundary (free volume per unit area)

  • vacuum (float) – Amount of vacuum to add at ends of cell - setting a non-zero value results in a supercell containing a single boundary

  • fix_blocks (float) – Length of regions in which to fix positions of atoms (region will be chosen most distant from the boundary or boundaries)

  • a (float) – Lattice parameter, defualts to 1.0

  • bicrystal_shifted (bool) – Flag indicating whether two halves of bicrystal are shifted relative to one antother at the boundary

  • boundary_plane_shifted (bool) – Flag indicating whether the nominal boundary plane between the two crystals has been shifter perpendicular to the boudnary plane

  • vacuum_set (bool) – Flag indicate whether vacuum has been added at th ends of the supercell (resulting in a single boundary and two free surfaces)

  • expansion_set (bool) – Flag indicates whether a volume expansion has been added at the boundary

  • num_atoms (int) – Number of atoms in the supercell

  • supercell_size_set (bool) – Flag indicates whether the size of the supercell has been set

  • normal_constraint (bool) – Flag indicates whether atoms are constrined to move only in direction normal to boundary plane (via format of certain output files)

  • blocks_fixed (bool) – Flag indicates whether blocks of atoms have been fixed (via format of certain output files)

  • debug (bool) – Flag to indicate whether debug information is requested

  • atom_arrays_calculated (bool) – Flag to indicate whether the atomic coordinates in the supercell have been calculated

  • boundary_plane_z (ndarray(2,dtype=float)) – Positions of the two grain boundaries (z-coordinates of boundary planes)

  • r_black (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Coordinates of atoms in black half of bicrystal

  • r_white (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Coordinates of atoms in white half of bicrystal

  • atom_types_black (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – Types of atoms in black half of bicrystal

  • atom_types_white (ndarray(N,,dtype=float)) – Types of atoms in white half of bicrystal

  • num_atoms_black (int) – Number of atoms in black half of bicrystal

  • num_atoms_white (int) – Number of atoms in white half of bicrystal

  • r_black_orig (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

  • r_white_orig (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

  • atom_types_black_orig (ndarray(N,dtype=float)) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

  • atom_types_white_orig (ndarray(N,,dtype=float)) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

  • num_atoms_black_orig (int) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

  • num_atoms_white_orig (int) – Copy of above variable used to make gamma surface calculations more efficient

__init__(grainboundary)

Initialise an empty supercell

Parameters:

grainboundary (object) – Grain boundary object for which a supercell is to be created

calculate_atom_arrays(tol=0.001, vis_type=None, gamma_surf=False, overfill=False)

Method for calculating the final atom arrays and supercell specification ready for use, and prior to writing out.

Note that this method does the majority of the work of this class. This makes the method large and complicated, but at least keeps all the logic for handling microscopic degrees of freedom, constraints, etc in one place, i.e. features like the vacuum layer, whether certain atoms are constrained, relative grain shifts and excess volume at the boundaries are all implemented in the process of calculating the atomic coordinates.

If debug information is requested, by calling set_debug(), then this method also checks for equivalence between the two boundaries and reports the result of the test.

A final complexity is that the method works slightly differently depending on whether it is called to build a single instance of a boundary or as part of a gamma surface calculation. If the latter, then the initial set of atomic coordinates, prior to the application of various types of microscopic adjustment, is retained in order to increase efficiency. See specification of the parameter gamma_surf, below.

Parameters:

  • tol (float, optional) – Tolerance for atom position tests, defaults to GBSUPERCELL_TOL

  • vis_type (str, optional) – Request visualisation for display in a Jupyter notebook. Can be ‘2d’ or ‘3d’, defaults to None

  • gamma_surf (bool, optional) – Set to True if this function is called from gbcalculation.gamma_surface_build(), defaults to False. This greatly increases the efficiency of gamma surface calculations by avoiding repeated recalculstation of the atom coordinates

  • overfill (bool, optional) – set to True to overfill the box. WARNING! useful for visualisations, but will probably break periodicity with periodic boundaries! Defaults to False

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Number of repeats in crystals must be set before filling with atoms”, if set_supercell_size() has not been called

  • RuntimeError – “Incorrect value for vis_type in gbsupercell.calculate_atom_arrays()”

calculate_rotation()

Calculate the rotation requiree to bring the grain boundary normal parallel withe the cartesian z axis. Also poultate details of the supercell cell vectors and the rotated form of the lattice basis vectors in each half of the bicrystal Reorders the description of the supercell in a format suitable for use in lammps input

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Error generating unit cell for supercell” if reordering of the basis vectors fails

get_supercell_definition()

Return details of a supercell. The atom_data array contains details of both black and white atoms. First three columns are the coordinates. The fourth column is the type.

Returns:

num_atoms, self.supercell, atom_data

Return type:

int, ndarray((3,3),dtype=float), ndarray((N,4),dtype=float)

set_bicrystal_shift(dsc_shift=None, inplane_shift=None)

Set the relative inter-granular translation at the boundary

Parameters:

  • dsc_shift (ndarray(3,dtype=float) or [float,float,float], optional) – Relative shift of grains at boundary as multiples of DSC lattice vectors, defaults to None

  • inplane_shift (ndarray(2,dtype=float) or [float,float], optional) – Relative shift of grains at boundary as multiples of the two supercell cell vectors in the GB plane, defaults to None

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Relative shift must be specified either as a multiple of dsc vectors or of in-plane csl vectors”, if neither paramter set

  • RuntimeError – “Relative crystal shift must have 2 components”

  • RuntimeError – “Shifts must be less than 1.0”

  • RuntimeError – “Relative shift vector should have zero z-component, something has gone wrong!”. This error should never be invoked if the code is working as intended

  • RuntimeError – “Relative crystal shift must have 3 components”

  • RuntimeError – “DSC basis must be calculated before setting shift in this way”

  • RuntimeError – “Shifts must be less than 1.0”

set_boundary_plane_shift(plane_shift)

Set the relative shift of the boundary plane in direction normal to plane Use with caution - there has been only limited testing of this functionality

Parameters:

plane_shift (float) – Shift the boundary plane perpendicular to plane, expressed as a fraction of repeat period based on DSC lattice.

set_debug()

Turn on debug info

set_expansion(expansion_discrete=None, expansion_sigmoid=None)

Set the expansion (excess volume per unit area) at each boundary in distance units

Parameters:

  • expansion_discrete (float, optional) – Expansion to apply at each boundary in distance units, defaults to None

  • expansion_sigmoid ([float,float], optional) – Parmeters of sigmoid function to determine a smooth distribution of excess volume at boundary, defaults to None

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Grain boundary expansion must be specified either as a single value for a discrete block expansion or as a pair of values for a sigmoid expansion”

  • RuntimeError – “Sigmoidal expansion method not currently implemented”

  • RuntimeError – “Specification of expansion sigmoid requires two numbers, the second larger than the first”, this error is not currently reachable

set_fix_block(fixblock)

Constrain atoms in block of width given in distance units to remain fixed

Parameters:

fixblock (float) – length of blocks of atoms to fix

set_lattice_parameter(a)

Set the value of the primary lattice parameter

Parameters:

a (float) – Primary lattice parameter

set_normal_constraint()

Constrain all atoms to move only in z-direction. Note that this will only affect certain file formats

set_supercell_size(repeats=None, radius=None)

Set the size of the simulation supercell as either a number of repeats of the minimal grain boundary cell or as a sphere radius (sphere method nod uccrently implemented)

Parameters:

  • repeats (ndarray(4,dtype=int) or [int,int,int,int], optional) – vector or list with four components, two specifiying number of repeats in GB plane and two more specifying repeats normal to plane in black and white halves, defaults to None

  • radius (float, optional) – Radius of spherical cluster, defaults to None

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Either number of repeats in crystal blocks or sphere radius must be specified”, if no parameter passed

  • RuntimeError – “Vector of repeats must have 4 components”, if specification of size has wrong number of components

  • RuntimeError – “Total supercell length must be an even number of repeats”

  • RuntimeError – “Spherical cluster case not currently supported”, if a radius for a spherical cluster has been specified

set_vacuum(vacuum)

Set the vacuum width at the end of the cell in distance units

Parameters:

vacuum (float) – Amount of vacuum to add at ends of supercell

unset_debug()

Turn off debug info

visualise_2d()

Generate a 2D plot showing the Supercell geometry

visualise_3d()

Generate a 3D plot showing the Supercell geometry

write_lammps(filename='lammps.txt', skew=True)

Write out the supercell in Lammps format

Parameters:

  • filename (str, optional) – Name of file to write to, defaults to ‘lammps.txt’

  • skew (bool, optional) – Flag to indicate if a highly skewed supercell is permitted (True) or if skew should be minimised (False), defaults to True

write_vasp(filename='vasp.txt', fix_xy=False)

Write out the supercell in Vasp POSCAR format

Parameters:

  • filename (str, optional) – Name of file to write to, defaults to ‘vasp.txt’

  • fix_xy (bool, optional) – Flag to request selective dynamics and constrain particles to move only in z-direction (perpendicualr to boundary plane), defaults to False

gbtk.grainboundary module

Module to handle definitions of grain boundaries. A boundary is described by 5 macroscopic degrees of freedom. It may or may not have a CSL associated with the axis-angle combination

Author: Chris Race
Date: 4th January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.grainboundary.GrainBoundary(latticetype, lengths=None, angles=None)

Bases: object

A grain holds details of the misorientation axis-angle combination and the boundary plane for a grain boundary. Where the axis-angle combination corresponds to a CSL it will also contain the csl object

Variables:

  • lattice (object) – lattice object defining lattice in which grain boundary exists

  • axis ([int,int,int]) – Misorientation axis for grain boundary

  • angle (float) – Misorientation angle (in radians)

  • angle_indices (ndarray(2,dtype=int)) – specification of misorientation angle via a pair of integers m.n

  • csl (object) – csl object defining the coincident site lattice for the grain boundary

  • boundary_normal_black (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Normal to boundary in black half of bicrystal, expressed in multiples of lattice basis vectors

  • boundary_normal_white (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Normal to boundary in white half of bicrystal, expressed in multiples of lattice basis vectors

  • boundary_normal_cartesian_black (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Normal to boundary in black half of bicrystal, in cartesian coordinates

  • boundary_normal_cartesian_white (ndarray(3,dtype=float)) – Normal to boundary in white half of bicrystal, in cartesian coordinates

  • boundary_indices (ndarray(3,dtype=int)) – Boundary plane specification as multiples of the three CSL cell vectors

  • boundary_cell_csl (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system, vectors of edges in rows, expressed in multiples of CSL cell vectors

  • boundary_cell_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system, in black system, vectors of edges in rows, expressed in multiples of lattice vectors

  • boundary_cell_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system, in white system, vectors of edges in rows, expressed in multiples of lattice vectors

  • boundary_cell_cartesian_black (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system, in black system, vectors of edges in rows, expressed in cartesians

  • boundary_cell_cartesian_white (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – Smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system, in white system, vectors of edges in rows, expressed in cartesians

  • cell_volume (float) – Volume of smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system

  • cell_volume_csl (float) – Number of CSL cells in smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system

  • cell_volume_lattice (float) – Number of lattice cells in smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system

  • cell_volume_atoms (float) – Number of atoms in smallest repeat unit for bicrystal system

  • boundary_type (string) – Broad boundary class - twist, tilt, symmetric (tilt) or mixed

  • search (boolean)

  • debug (boolean) – Flag set to True if debug information requested

  • angle_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if misorientation angle is set

  • axis_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if misorientation axis is set

  • csl_basis_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if csl_basis has been calculated (and csl_vectors_black, etc. populated)

  • boundary_plane_set (boolean) – Flag to indicate if boundary plane has been set and normal vectors calculated.

__init__(latticetype, lengths=None, angles=None)

Initialise an empty grain boundary object with the specified lattice type

Parameters:

  • latticetype (string) – Lattice type, passed to Lattice module

  • lengths (list of floats, optional) – Lengths of unit cell vectors, passed to Lattice module, defaults to None

  • angles (list of floats, optional) – Angles between unit cell vectors, passed to Lattice module, defaults to None

set_angle(angle)

Set the misorientation angle directly with a value in radians

Parameters:

angle (float) – Angle specified in radians

set_angle_mn(m, n)

Set the misorientation angle via a pair of integers. Only works for cubic lattice types

Parameters:

  • m (integer) – first parameter

  • n (integer) – second parameter

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Axis must be specified before setting angle using this method” if axis not already specified

  • RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + self.lattice.lattice_type + “ not supported.” if an unsupported lattice type is specified

set_axis(axis)

_summary_

Parameters:

axis ([int,int,int]) – Components of axis specified in multiples of the lattice cell vectors

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Misorientation axis vector must have 3 components” if wrong number of components specified

set_boundary_plane_csl(boundary_indices, orthogonal=False, tol=0.001, search_radius=10, min_angle=0.5235987755982988, suppress_error=False)

Set the boundary plane for a boundary with a csl. Plane is specified by components of plane normal in the CSL basis

Parameters:

  • boundary_indices ([int,int,int]) – List (or array) of three indices specifying the bounday plane normal in multiples of the CSL basis vectors

  • orthogonal (bool, optional) – Flag specifiying whether an orthogonal grain boundary cell is required, defaults to False

  • tol (float, optional) – Tolerance to use for determing orthogonality or non-equivalence of vectors, defaults to GRAINBOUNDARY_TOL

  • search_radius (int, optional) – Radius to use in search for basis vectors, defaults to 10

  • min_angle (float, optional) – Minimum angle to allow between grain boundary cell vectors (sets limit on extent of skewing), defaults to 30 degrees

  • suppress_error (bool, optional) – Allows exception on failing to find a set of cell vectors to be ignored. Useful for catalogueing, defaults to False

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “csl basis must be set before setting boundary plane via this method”, if attempting to specify plane, without first setting up CSL. Run set_csl() first.

  • RuntimeError – “Could not find first in-plane vector - consider increasing search_radius”, if no vector in GB plane can be found within search bounds

  • RuntimeError – “Could not find second in-plane vector - consider increasing search_radius”, if second vector in GB plane can be found within search bounds (which may also be required to be orthogonal to first)

  • RuntimeError – “No vector out of boundary plane could be found - consider increasing search_radius”, if the final vector cannot be found (which may also be required to be orthogonal to other two)

Returns:

Flag indicating success of attempt to define GB cell

Return type:

bool

set_csl(csl_vectors_black=None, csl_vectors_white=None, search_limit=None)

_summary_

Parameters:

  • csl_vectors_black (ndarray((3,3), dtype=float), optional) – Basis vectors of CSL cell in black crystal, defaults to None - PROBABLY NOT CURRENTLY IMPLEMENTED

  • csl_vectors_white (ndarray((3,3), dtype=float), optional) – Basis vectors of CSL cell in white crystal, defaults to None - PROBABLY NOT CURRENTLY IMPLEMENTED

  • search_limit (integer, optional) – Limit for spatial search used to find CSL cell, defaults to None

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Axis and angle must be set before setting csl basis”, if either the misorientation axis or angle are not currently set

set_debug()

Turn on debug info

unset_debug()

Turn off debug info

visualise_3d()

Generate a plot showing the Grain boundary geometry

visualise_3d_rotated()

Generate a plot showing the Grain boundary geometry

gbtk.grainboundary.dedupe_catalogue(gb_df, lattice_type)

Remove duplicate grain boundaries from a catalogue dataframe, based on symmetric equivalency i underlying crystal lattice

Parameters:

  • gb_df (Pandas dataframe) – Grain boundry catalogue dataframe to dedupe

  • lattice_type (string) – Lattice type. Can be ‘sc’, ‘bcc’, ‘fcc’

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Cataloguing functionality not available for “ + lattice_type + “ lattice type”, if unsupported lattice type specified

Returns:

Dataframe without duplicates

Return type:

Pandas dataframe

gbtk.grainboundary.write_gb_catalogue(folder, lattice_type, axis, m, n, limit, ext='df', atom_limit=None, csl_limit=5, orthogonal=False, search_radius=10)

Write out a catalogue of possible grain boundary planes for a given axis-angle combination

Parameters:

  • folder (string) – Location of folder to write catalogue to

  • lattice_type (string) – Lattice type. Can be ‘sc’, ‘bcc’, ‘fcc’

  • axis ([int,int,int] or ndarray(3, dtype=int)) – Misorientation axis to consider

  • m (int) – First parameter in pair (m,n) used to specify a misorientation angle. Matches values output in a CSL catalogue

  • n (int) – Second parameter in pair (m,n) used to specify a misorientation angle. Matches values output in a CSL catalogue

  • limit (int) – Limit on values of indices specifying grain boundary plane

  • ext (str, optional) – Extension for file output (controls file type), defaults to ‘df’. Can be ‘df’, ‘csv’, ‘xlsx’

  • atom_limit (int, optional) – Maximum allowable nummber of atoms in grain boundary cell, defaults to None

  • csl_limit (int, optional) – Search limit applied in finding CSL cell, defaults to 5. Increasing this will slow things down but increase success rate of search

  • orthogonal (bool, optional) – Flag determines if vectors defining GB cell forced to be mutually orthogonal, defaults to False

  • search_radius (int, optional) – Search limit used in setting grain boundary plane, defaults to 10

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Cataloguing functionality not available for “ + lattice_type + “ lattice type”, if unsupported lattice type specified

  • RuntimeError – “Unsupported file type: “ + ext + “. Extention must be one of: df, csv, xlsx”

Returns:

Grain boundary catalogue

Return type:

Pandas dataframe

gbtk.lattice module

Module to store and manipulate crystal lattices

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.lattice.Lattice(lattice_type, lengths=None, angles=None)

Bases: object

A lattice holds a description of the basic crystal structure. This description includes the vectors defining the edges of the unit cell and the fractional coordinates of the atoms within that cell.

Variables:

  • lattice_type (string) – Type of lattice. Can be fcc, bcc, sc, fcc-bct, hcp, hcp-ortho, CsCl, ZrO2-fct

  • cell_vectors (ndarray((3,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array storing vectors defining unit cell parallelipiped in rows

  • basis_coords (ndarray((num_basis,3),dtype=float)) – Basis - positions of atoms in unit cell in fractional coordinates (fractions of cell_vectors)

  • num_basis (integer) – Number of atoms in basis

  • atom_types (ndarray(num_basis,dtype=int)) – Types of atoms in basis, specified as consecutive integers indicating differnt types from 1 to num_atom_types

  • num_atom_types (int) – Number of atom types in unit cell

  • symmetries (object) – Symmetries of the lattice type, currently only populated for fcc lattice type

__init__(lattice_type, lengths=None, angles=None)

Initialises a lattice of a specified type and dimensions Dimensions are relative to the a-direction cell length Shape of the cell is specified by a series of optional values for lengths and angles. The number of these values will be different for different lattice types as follows:

fcc, bcc, sc, fcc-bct, CsCl: 0

hcp, hcp-ortho, ZrO2-fct: 0 => ideal, 1 => c/a ratio,

Parameters:

  • lattice_type (string) – Type of lattice. Can be fcc, bcc, sc, fcc-bct, hcp, hcp-ortho, CsCl, ZrO2-fct

  • lengths (list of floats, optional) – Lengths of unit cell vectors, defaults to None

  • angles (list of floats, optional) – Angles between unit cell vectors, defaults to None

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Cell shape specification not required for latticetype”, if angles or lengths specified when neither required

  • RuntimeError – “Cell angle specification not required for latticetype”, if angles specified when not required

  • RuntimeError – “If specifying cell shape specify only c/a ratio for latticetype”, if shape specified, but incorrect number of lengths given

  • RuntimeError – “Unknown lattice type”, if unsupported value specified for lattice_type

copy_lattice(lattice)

Copy attributes from supplied lattice object

Parameters:

lattice (lattice object) – lattice object to copy attributes from

print_lattice_spec()

Print details of lattice object

visualise_3d()

Generate a plot showing the lattice unit cell

gbtk.lattice.print_supported_types()

gbtk.spatialsearch module

Module to define a spatialseaerch object for increasing the efficiency of grid-based searches

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.spatialsearch.SpatialSearch(maxcomponent, basis_vectors=None, dimension=3)

Bases: object

A spatialsearch object holds an array of position vectors on a grid defined by integer multiples of a set of basis vectors in cartesian space within a circle or sphere of a given radius and their distances from the origin, sorted by distance. Note that these distances assume a cubic basis. Application in a non cubic system will mean that vectors are not considered in completely correct size order.

The purpose of this object is to speed up grid-based searches

Variables:

  • space (nrarray((nspace,[3,4]),dtype=float)) – Numpy array containing vectors and lengths (in fourth column) of those vectors from origin to points on a simple square or cubic grid

  • nspace (int) – Number of vectors in array

__init__(maxcomponent, basis_vectors=None, dimension=3)

Populates the array of search points in a spatialsearch object

Parameters:

  • maxcomponent (int) – Radius of search sphere. Corresponds to largest component of any vectors returned

  • basis_vectors (ndarray((3,3), dtype=float), optional) – Array of basis vectors in rows, defaults to None, in which case a square grid (cubic basis) is assumed

  • dimension (int, optional) – dimensionality of space, defaults to 3

Raises:

RuntimeError – “Dimension for search space must be 2 or 3” if incorrect value for dimension specified

gbtk.symmetry module

Module to define and store symmetry operations of crystal lattices

Author: Chris Race
Date: 3rd January 2017
Contact: christopher.race@manchester.ac.uk
class gbtk.symmetry.Symmetry(lattice_type)

Bases: object

Calculates and stores a set of symmettry operations (as matrices) for a given crystal lattice

Variables:

  • mirror_planes (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array plane normals of N mirror planes

  • rot_2 (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array storing axes of N two-fold rotations

  • rot_3 (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array storing axes of N three-fold rotations

  • rot_4 (ndarray((N,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array storing axes of N four-fold rotations

  • symops (ndarray((N,3,3),dtype=float)) – numpy array storing transformation matrices for full set of N syymetry operations (including identity and inversion)

  • n_trans (integer) – Number of symmetry operations in symops

  • equiv_vecs – CODE CURRENTLY COMMENTED OUT Probably defunct

  • symops_set (boolean) – Set to true when symmetry operations calcualated (i.e. when symops populated)

__init__(lattice_type)

Initialises the symmetry properties lattice of a specified type. Currently supported types are: fcc, sc, bcc

Parameters:

lattice_type (string) – lattice type to consider

Raises:

  • RuntimeError – “Lattice type must be specified.” where no lattice type specified

  • RuntimeError – “Lattice type “ + lattice_type + “ not supported.” where specified lattice type not supported

vector_equiv(v1, v2, tol=0.001)

Check to see if two vectors are symmetrically equivalent

Module contents
